mnemonics.title: CIA Triad Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Think of CIA as 'Confidential Info Always protected' to remember the three pillars: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.

mnemonics.title: Attack Lifecycle Steps
mnemonics.content: Remember the attack phases with: 'Rats Gave Mice Cheese' - Reconnaissance, Scanning, Gaining Access, Maintaining Access, Covering Tracks.

mnemonics.title: Types of Malware
mnemonics.content: Use 'RITS' - Ransomware, Infectors (viruses/worms), Trojans, Spyware to remember major malware types.

acronyms.usage: Core principles of information security forming the foundation of security policies.
Attack that floods target with traffic from multiple sources to overwhelm resources.
Attack where attacker intercepts communication between two parties secretly.

acronyms.expansion: Intrusion Detection System / Intrusion Prevention System
acronyms.usage: Security tools that monitor network traffic for suspicious activities.
Creates secure encrypted connection over public networks for privacy.
Programming language used for database management and queries.
Attack that injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a three-legged stool representing Confidentiality (lock), Integrity (checkmark), and Availability (uptime/clock) - all three legs are essential for stability.

Imagine a thief's workflow: 1) Case the joint (Reconnaissance), 2) Check windows/doors (Scanning), 3) Break in (Gaining Access), 4) Install hidden cameras (Maintaining Access), 5) Wear gloves/avoid cameras (Covering Tracks).
Picture a castle wall with a gate - the firewall filters what's allowed in and out, just like guards at the castle entrance.
Imagine a fisher casting a baited hook - attackers use convincing lures (emails) hoping you'll bite and reveal your credentials.

analogies.analogy: Malware is like germs/viruses causing illness, while antivirus is like the immune system or vaccines that detect and neutralize these threats.
Authentication is like showing your ID at airport security (proving who you are), while authorization is like having a boarding pass for first class (determining what you can access).
A firewall is like a security guard at a building entrance - checking IDs, allowing or denying entry based on predefined rules.
Encryption is like speaking a secret language only you and the recipient understand - even if someone overhears, they can't understand the message.
Zero-day is like a secret flaw in your house only a thief knows about - by the time you discover it, they could have already broken in.
